citations.narrative:
The enclosure for 9 Russian dwarf hamsters had sharp wire points by their water and food rack that hamsters could touch
when they eat/drink. Surfaces for hamsters must be smooth and sharp wire points could injure the hamsters. Ensure to
maintain surfaces in good repair with smooth surfaces to protect hamsters from injury.

A water bottle for 5 guinea pigs had a greenish build-up seen when looking down on the enclosure. The buildup was
touched and could not be wiped off from the outside and confirmed to be inside the bottle. Green build-up in a water bottle
is a sign that it is dirty and dirty water bottles could make guinea pigs sick. Ensure to clean and sanitize water bottles at
least every two weeks but sooner if they are dirty.

The facility does not maintain an adequate level of housekeeping for the room which houses guinea pigs and hamsters. A
table used to box guinea pigs and hamsters for shipping has rodent feces, used bedding, and dust scattered throughout
the table surface. A ring of debris and rodent feces outlines a five-gallon plastic bucket locating on the table, indicating
this table has not been cleaned recently. According to the facility, the floor of the guinea pig and hamster room was
recently mopped. Inspectors observed dirt spanning multiple linoleum tiles adhered to the floor in several locations of the
room.
Areas that are not kept clean and in good repair may promote bacterial growth or encourage pests, and therefore may
subsequently impact overall animal health and lead to disease.
The building and grounds shall be kept clean and in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to facilitate the
prescribed husbandry practices set forth in this subpart, as well as remain free of accumulations of trash.
The licensee must demonstrate full compliance with the Act, regulations, and standards within 1 more inspections or by
March 20, 2026, or the applicant will forfeit the license fee and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your
inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until March 20, 2026.
Additional Inspectors:
SCOTT WELCH, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
3.28(a)(1) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
The enclosure for 9 Russian dwarf hamsters had sharp wire points by their water and food rack that hamsters could touch 
when they eat/drink. Surfaces for hamsters must be smooth and sharp wire points could injure the hamsters. Ensure to 
maintain surfaces in good repair with smooth surfaces to protect hamsters from injury. 
3.30 
   
Watering. 
A water bottle for 5 guinea pigs had a greenish build-up seen when looking down on the enclosure. The buildup was 
touched and could not be wiped off from the outside and confirmed to be inside the bottle. Green build-up in a water bottle 
is a sign that it is dirty and dirty water bottles could make guinea pigs sick. Ensure to clean and sanitize water bottles at 
least every two weeks but sooner if they are dirty. 
3.31(b) 
   
Sanitation. 
The facility does not maintain an adequate level of housekeeping for the room which houses guinea pigs and hamsters. A 
table used to box guinea pigs and hamsters for shipping has rodent feces, used bedding, and dust scattered throughout 
the table surface. A ring of debris and rodent feces outlines a five-gallon plastic bucket locating on the table, indicating 
this table has not been cleaned recently. According to the facility, the floor of the guinea pig and hamster room was 
recently mopped. Inspectors observed dirt spanning multiple linoleum tiles adhered to the floor in several locations of the 
room.  
Areas that are not kept clean and in good repair may promote bacterial growth or encourage pests, and therefore may 
subsequently impact overall animal health and lead to disease. 
 
The building and grounds shall be kept clean and in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to facilitate the 
prescribed husbandry practices set forth in this subpart, as well as remain free of accumulations of trash. 
 
The licensee must demonstrate full compliance with the Act, regulations, and standards within 1 more inspections or by 
March 20, 2026, or the applicant will forfeit the license fee and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your

inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until March 20, 2026. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
SCOTT WELCH, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
